# Electrical Plugs & Sockets in Rwanda: Types C, E, and G

> A guide to electrical plug and socket types used in Rwanda. Rwanda uses Types C, E, and G at 230V/50Hz. Includes the Type J database error correction.

Rwanda uses plug types C, E, and G at 230V / 50Hz. This site covers Rwanda's electrical standards, plug compatibility for travellers, and documents a widely propagated database error that incorrectly listed Type J (Swiss) as a Rwandan plug type.

## Key Facts

- Voltage: 230 V
- Frequency: 50 Hz
- Plug types in use: C, E, G
- Official standard: Type G (British BS 1363), adopted as part of East African Community (EAC) harmonisation with Kenya, Tanzania, and Uganda
- Compatible with most of Europe and Africa — no voltage converter needed for devices rated 230V/50Hz

## Plug Types

- Type C (Europlug): Compact, ungrounded two-pin plug, 4.0 mm diameter pins. Used for low-power devices like chargers, laptops, and small appliances. Fits directly into Type E sockets found throughout Rwanda. Dedicated Type C sockets are very rare.
- Type E (French/Belgian): Grounded plug where the socket provides the grounding pin, 4.8 mm diameter pins. Once Rwanda's standard, still the most widespread grounded socket in older buildings. Some homes built in the late 2010s still use Type E.
- Type F (Schuko): Two round pins with grounding clips on socket sides. Listed by the IEC for Rwanda but uncommon in practice — mostly encountered through imported European appliances.
- Type G (British BS 1363): Three rectangular pins in triangular pattern, with built-in fuse at 13 A. Rwanda's official standard, adopted as part of East African Community harmonisation. Required in all new construction since the early 2010s.

## Plug Compatibility

- European 2-pin (Type C) plugs: Compatible — work in Type E sockets, common in older buildings.
- French/Belgian (Type E) plugs: Compatible — work in Type E sockets, common in older buildings.
- British (Type G) plugs: Compatible — sockets found in newer buildings and hotels. May also be found in some older buildings.
- US, Canadian, Chinese, Japanese, Australian, Indian, and other non-European plug types: Adapter required.
- A universal travel adapter is recommended for the best coverage across both older and newer buildings.

## How to Choose a Travel Adapter for Rwanda

1. Check your plug type: Identify your device's plug type. European two-pin (Type C) plugs work directly in Type E sockets found in older buildings, without an adapter.
2. Check the building age: Older Rwandan buildings use Type E (French/Belgian) sockets. Newer buildings and hotels use Type G (British) sockets, Rwanda's official standard adopted as part of EAC harmonisation.
3. Get the right adapter: US, Australian, and other non-European travellers will need an adapter. UK travellers may find Type G sockets in newer buildings, but should carry an adapter for older buildings with Type E sockets. A universal travel adapter is recommended for the best coverage.
4. Check your device voltage: Rwanda uses 230V at 50Hz. Most modern electronics (laptops, phones) support 100-240V and work without a converter. Check your device's label to confirm.

## Type J Database Error

Some international databases, notably those derived from IEC data, incorrectly listed Rwanda as using plug types C and J. Type J is the Swiss plug, used exclusively in Switzerland and Liechtenstein. This was a data entry error that propagated across reference databases. The IEC corrected Rwanda's listing in 2025 to types C, E, F, and G, removing the erroneous Type J.

## Frequently Asked Questions

Q: What plug types are used in Rwanda?
A: Rwanda uses plug types C, E, and G. Type C (Europlug) fits directly into Type E sockets and is used for low-power devices like chargers and laptops. Dedicated Type C sockets are very rare. Type E (French/Belgian) was the former standard and remains the most common grounded socket in older buildings. Type G (British) is Rwanda's official standard, adopted as part of East African Community harmonisation, and now required in all new construction.

Q: What voltage and frequency does Rwanda use?
A: Rwanda uses 230 V at 50 Hz, the same standard as most of Europe and Africa. No voltage converter is needed for devices rated for this standard.

Q: Why do some websites list Type J for Rwanda?
A: Some international databases, notably those derived from IEC data, incorrectly listed Rwanda as using plug types C and J. Type J is the Swiss plug, used primarily in Switzerland and Liechtenstein. This was a data entry error that propagated across reference databases. It has since been corrected in IEC and other sources, though a few secondary sites still show the old data.

Q: Do I need a travel adapter for Rwanda?
A: It depends on where you're from. European two-pin (Type C) plugs work directly in Type E sockets, which are common in older buildings. US, Australian, and other non-European travellers will need an adapter. A universal travel adapter is recommended for the best coverage across both older and newer buildings.

Q: I'm from the UK — do I need an adapter for Rwanda?
A: Type G (British) is Rwanda's official standard, so many newer buildings, hotels, and commercial properties will have Type G sockets. However, older buildings may only have Type E (French/Belgian) sockets, which are not compatible with Type G plugs. It's always a good idea to carry an adapter just in case your accommodation doesn't have Type G sockets.

Q: What is the East African Community (EAC) plug standard?
A: Rwanda has adopted Type G (British BS 1363) as its official plug standard, as part of East African Community harmonisation. Along with Kenya, Tanzania, and Uganda, Rwanda now requires Type G sockets in all new electrical installations. This means Type G is becoming increasingly common, especially in Kigali and other urban centres.

## Sources

### Corrected sources

- Rwanda Standards Board — DEAS 495-2 specification for Type G (EAS 495) socket-outlets (rsb.gov.rw)
- Rwanda Standards Board — Public Review Notice #41 listing EAS 495-1 through 495-4 (rsb.gov.rw)
- IEC World Plugs (iec.ch/world-plugs) — lists C, E, F, G (corrected 2025, removing Type J)
- worldstandards.eu — lists C, E, F, G
- Wikipedia: Energy in Rwanda — lists C, E, G
- Wikivoyage: Rwanda — lists C, E, G
- Wise (wise.com/gb/plug-types/rwanda) — lists C, E, F, G
- Power Plugs & Sockets World (powerplugsockets.world/country/rwanda) — lists C, E, F, G
- World Power Plugs (world-power-plugs.com/rwanda) — lists C, E, F, G

### Sources still showing incorrect data

- power-plugs-sockets.com — still lists C, J (incorrect)
- Electrical Safety First (electricalsafetyfirst.org.uk) — still lists C, J (incorrect)

## About This Site

Rwanda Plugs & Sockets (https://plugs.rw) is a reference guide to electrical plug and socket types used in Rwanda. The site covers Rwanda's electrical standards, plug compatibility for travellers, and documents the Type J database error correction.

- Website: https://plugs.rw
- Region: Rwanda (RW)
- Language: English
